_meta.description: Comparison of business structures available to Quebec micro-entrepreneurs. Used by the roadmap and assistant to explain trade-offs.

structures.plain_language_summary: The simplest way to start. You and your business are legally the same entity. Most micro-entrepreneurs in Quebec start here. You register with the REQ and get an NEQ number, then operate under your own name or a trade name.
best_for: Anyone testing a business idea before committing

registration.cost_note: One-time registration fee as of 2026. Annual update is $38.
registration.timeline: Same day online, 1-3 business days by mail

taxes.summary: Business income flows directly onto your personal tax return (T1/TP1). You pay personal income tax rates, not corporate rates.
taxes.income_tax: Personal rates (14–25.75% Quebec + 15–33% federal, after abatement)
taxes.qpp: 12.8% on net business income (both employee + employer shares)
taxes.gst_qst: Must register and collect once revenue exceeds $30,000/year
taxes.tax_filing: File Schedule T2125 (federal) and TP-80 (Quebec) with personal return
taxes.installments: Required quarterly if expected tax owing > $3,000 federal or $1,800 Quebec

liability.summary: You are personally liable for all business debts and legal claims. Your personal assets (savings, car, home) can be seized if the business is sued.
liability.risk_note: Acceptable for low-risk businesses. Get business liability insurance to mitigate.

pros: Cheapest and fastest to set up ($42)
No separate corporate tax return — one personal return
Business losses reduce your personal taxable income
Easy to dissolve if things don't work out
cons: Full personal liability for business debts
Pays personal income tax rates (higher than small business corporate rate at higher incomes)
No income splitting with a spouse
Less credible to some corporate clients
structures.income_threshold_note: At net income above ~$80,000, a corporation often becomes more tax-efficient. Below that, sole proprietorship is usually simpler and cheaper overall.

structures.plain_language_summary: A separate legal entity from you personally. More complex and expensive to set up and maintain, but offers liability protection and significant tax advantages at higher incomes. Usually not necessary for a micro-business in the first year.
best_for: Businesses with significant liability risk (contractors working on client sites, professionals)
Business owners who want to retain earnings in the company
IT consultants and professionals with corporate clients requiring incorporation

registration.cost_lawyer: 500–2000 if using a lawyer (recommended for first-timers)

taxes.summary: Corporation pays its own tax at low small business rates. You pay personal tax only when you take money out as salary or dividends.
taxes.corporate_rate: ~12.2% on first $500,000 of active business income (Quebec CCPC)
taxes.personal_on_salary: Same as sole proprietorship — salary you pay yourself is taxed personally
taxes.personal_on_dividends: Dividends taxed at lower personal rates (dividend tax credit applies)
taxes.tax_filing: Separate T2 corporate return (federal) + CO-17 (Quebec) — requires an accountant (~$1,500–3,000/year)

liability.summary: The corporation is a separate legal entity. Your personal assets are generally protected from business debts and lawsuits.
liability.risk_note: Protection is not absolute — personal guarantees on loans and director liability for unpaid taxes/wages still apply

pros: Low corporate tax rate (~12.2%) allows tax deferral
Can retain earnings in the corporation and invest them
More credible to corporate clients and investors
Can split income with family shareholders (within limits)
cons: Much more expensive to set up ($367+ provincial or $200+ federal)
Requires annual corporate tax return ($1,500–3,000 in accounting fees)
More administrative burden (minutes, resolutions, shareholder registers)
Losses stay inside the corporation — can't offset personal income
structures.income_threshold_note: For most micro-entrepreneurs earning under $60,000–$80,000 net, the accounting costs of a corporation often outweigh the tax savings. Start as sole proprietor and incorporate when it makes financial sense.

structures.plain_language_summary: Two or more people operating a business together, sharing profits and liabilities. Each partner reports their share on their personal tax return. Must be registered with the REQ.
best_for: Professional partnerships (lawyers, accountants — some regulated professions require this)
Businesses where partners contribute different resources (one contributes capital, one contributes labour)

registration.partnership_agreement: Strongly recommended — a written agreement covering profit split, decision-making, exit terms. Not legally required but critical.

taxes.summary: Partnership itself doesn't pay tax. Each partner reports their share of income on their personal return.
taxes.qpp: Each partner pays QPP on their share of business income
taxes.filing: Partnership files an information return; each partner files personal return with their share

liability.summary: Each partner is jointly and personally liable for ALL partnership debts — even debts created by your partner without your knowledge.
liability.risk_note: Choose your partners carefully. Consider a limited partnership or LLP for liability protection.

pros: Simple to set up (same cost as sole proprietorship)
Shared financial burden of startup costs
cons: Full personal liability for all partnership debts, including partner's actions
Disputes between partners can destroy the business
All partners must agree on major decisions (unless agreement says otherwise)
Harder to add or remove partners

decision_guide.question_1: Is your expected net income under $80,000/year?
decision_guide.if_yes: Register a partnership or incorporate together. Do NOT just work informally — if one person gets sued or leaves, you need legal clarity.
decision_guide.if_no: Consider incorporation — the tax savings at higher incomes often justify the accounting costs.
decision_guide.question_2: Do you have significant liability risk (working on client property, giving advice that could cause harm, handling food for the public)?
decision_guide.question_3: Are you starting with a co-founder?
decision_guide.default_recommendation_hackathon: For most users of this tool, sole proprietorship is the right first step. They can always incorporate later.

free_resources.name: YES Montréal — Business Structure Advising
free_resources.note: Free one-on-one advice sessions for entrepreneurs, including help choosing a business structure
